critic_raw:
This experiment tests a CNN-LSTM hybrid versus logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction in chronic disease patients.

The 180-day lookback window for feature construction is described as providing temporal features (prior admission counts in rolling 30/60/90-day windows, time since last admission). However, for patients admitted early in their follow-up history, the 180-day lookback will contain limited or no history, making their sequences structurally different from patients with complete lookbacks. Zero-padding or truncation of these short sequences is not described. If early patients are disproportionately represented in the training set (years 1–3) and longer-history patients in the test set (years 4–5), the model learns primarily from short-history patients but is evaluated on long-history patients, introducing a structural covariate shift not addressed by stratification.

The hypothesis targets the contribution of 'temporal architecture' (the CNN-LSTM) over logistic regression. The logistic regression receives the 'identical feature set' after preprocessing. But the CNN-LSTM's 1D CNN processes temporal sequences, extracting local patterns from the sequence structure — this is qualitatively different input information from the aggregated rolling window statistics available to logistic regression. The hypothesis cannot be answered by this design because the CNN-LSTM and logistic regression are not processing the same information; the CNN-LSTM has access to the raw sequence ordering within the 180-day window, while logistic regression only has access to aggregated statistics.

The success criterion is stated as '≥0.03 absolute improvement in AUROC.' This threshold is arbitrary and post-hoc: 0.03 AUROC is clinically meaningful but statistically marginal. Without a pre-specified power calculation, this threshold may have been chosen because it is achievable given the expected variance of the test set AUROC estimate (approximately 6,000–10,000 test records). No minimum detectable effect size analysis is described.

Calibration is mentioned as a secondary metric but the design notes the model 'makes predictions conditional on observed care patterns — not counterfactual predictions.' In practice, if high-follow-up patients are systematically less likely to be readmitted (because more follow-up leads to intervention), the model will learn to predict low risk for high-follow-up patients. In deployment, this calibration transfers only if care intensity patterns remain the same. A calibration analysis that does not stratify by follow-up intensity will mask this dependency.
all_issues_raised: Patients admitted early in the study period have shorter lookback windows than patients later in the period. If training data overrepresents short-history sequences and test data overrepresents long-history sequences, the model faces structural covariate shift unrelated to disease progression patterns.

The CNN-LSTM processes raw sequence order within the 180-day window while logistic regression receives only rolling window aggregates. This is a qualitative information asymmetry, not just an architectural difference — the hypothesis that 'temporal architecture' adds value cannot be tested without matching the information content.

The ≥0.03 AUROC success criterion is not derived from a power calculation. Without pre-specified sample size justification, this threshold may be retrospectively calibrated to the expected noise level rather than to clinical significance.

Calibration conditioned on care follow-up intensity will be confounded: high-follow-up patients appear low-risk because intervention prevents readmission, not because the model correctly identifies low-risk patients. Stratified calibration by follow-up intensity is not described.
all_issues_adjudicated: Patients admitted early in the study period have shorter lookback windows than patients later in the period. If training data overrepresents short-history sequences and test data overrepresents long-history sequences, the model faces structural covariate shift unrelated to disease progression patterns.

The CNN-LSTM processes raw sequence order within the 180-day window while logistic regression receives only rolling window aggregates. This is a qualitative information asymmetry, not just an architectural difference — the hypothesis that 'temporal architecture' adds value cannot be tested without matching the information content.

The ≥0.03 AUROC success criterion is not derived from a power calculation. Without pre-specified sample size justification, this threshold may be retrospectively calibrated to the expected noise level rather than to clinical significance.

Calibration conditioned on care follow-up intensity will be confounded: high-follow-up patients appear low-risk because intervention prevents readmission, not because the model correctly identifies low-risk patients. Stratified calibration by follow-up intensity is not described.
